title: Google's Genkit Ships Agents API with Detached Turns and Human-in-the-Loop for TypeScript and Go
gist: Google shipped a preview Agents API for Genkit that unifies one-shot replies, streamed multi-turn chat, human-approval-gated tool calls, and detached long-running tasks behind a single chat() interface, while Google simultaneously builds a second, Google-hosted competing layer (Managed Agents in the Gemini API) -- positioning Genkit's self-hosted, model-agnostic, full-stack approach against a crowded field including LangChain, CrewAI, and Semantic Kernel.

insights.claim: Genkit's Agents API packages message history, the tool execution loop, streaming, state persistence, and a frontend protocol behind a single chat() interface that works identically whether an agent runs in-process or behind an HTTP endpoint, on the design principle that one abstraction should scale up rather than requiring developers to swap framework primitives as agent complexity grows from a simple chatbot to a multi-agent workflow.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: A concrete architectural bet -- one unifying abstraction instead of composed point-tools -- that other agent-framework vendors will be compared against as the space matures.
insights.locator: The design principle is one abstraction that scales up

insights.claim: Genkit separates 'custom state' (typed application data driving the next turn, e.g. workflow status or task lists) from 'artifacts' (generated outputs the user may inspect/download/version, e.g. a report or code patch), with tools updating either through the active session and changes streamed to the client as they happen.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: A reusable data-modeling distinction for anyone architecting agent session state, separating control-flow data from user-facing deliverables.
insights.locator: Genkit separates two kinds of agent data

insights.claim: 'Detached turns' let a client start an agent task, disconnect, and poll for results later while the agent continues working server-side, writing progress to snapshots any client can read -- making long research jobs, multi-step planning, and tool-heavy workflows practical without WebSockets, a separate job queue, or holding a connection open.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Removes significant infrastructure complexity (no WebSockets/job queue needed) for teams building long-running autonomous agent tasks, a common pain point in agent product architecture.
insights.locator: Detached turns let a client start an agent task

insights.claim: 'Interruptible tools' pause agent execution mid-run for human approval, returning the pending action to the client and validating the resume payload against session history before continuing, which specifically prevents a tool from being tricked into running with forged input.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: A concrete anti-prompt-injection/anti-forgery safety mechanism for human-in-the-loop agentic tool execution, relevant to enterprise risk assessments of autonomous agent deployments.
insights.locator: The other capability interruptible tools provide human-in-the-loop control

insights.claim: Server-managed state persistence (via a configured session store such as Firestore) enables reconnect-by-session-ID durability, while client-managed state (no server persistence) is explicitly positioned for ephemeral sessions or strict data-residency requirements, at the cost of growing network payload size as the conversation lengthens.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Gives regulated/EU enterprises a concrete architectural lever (client-managed state) to keep AI agent conversation data off the server entirely for compliance reasons.
insights.quote: This approach is ideal for ephemeral sessions or applications with strict data residency constraints where the server should not persist user data. The tradeoff is increased network payload size as the conversation grows.

insights.claim: Google is simultaneously building two layers of agent infrastructure -- Genkit for self-hosted agent apps and Managed Agents in the Gemini API for a fully Google-hosted runtime (background execution, remote MCP servers, sandboxed code execution) -- suggesting Google is hedging on whether developers will prefer to self-host or consume agent infrastructure as a managed service.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Signals internal strategic ambiguity at Google about the winning deployment model for agent infrastructure, relevant to assessing platform risk for developers choosing between Google's two competing agent stacks.
insights.locator: Google itself is building agent infrastructure at two layers simultaneously

insights.claim: Genkit competes in a crowded agent-framework field (LangChain, CrewAI, Semantic Kernel, Autogen, Mastra, Pydantic AI) and differentiates via a full-stack approach -- server-side agent logic, typed client SDKs for web/mobile, built-in streaming protocol, and deployment to Firebase/Cloud Run/any Node.js or Go environment -- but its tradeoff is a smaller, younger community-integration ecosystem than LangChain's.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Positions Genkit's competitive moat (full-stack integration) against its main weakness (ecosystem maturity) for teams evaluating agent-framework vendor choice.
insights.locator: The competitive context is a crowded field
